questions.question: Define steganography and explain how it differs from cryptography.
keyPoints: Steganography is the practice of hiding the existence of a message within another medium (image, audio, text).
Cryptography makes message content unreadable but the existence of encrypted communication is obvious.
Steganography makes the message invisible so observers do not even know communication is occurring.
Cryptography protects content; steganography conceals existence.

questions.question: Explain the LSB (Least Significant Bit) steganography technique for images. How does it work and what are its limitations?
keyPoints: LSB replaces the least significant bit of each pixel's color value with a bit of the secret message.
Since the LSB contributes only 1 to the pixel value (out of 0-255 range), the visual change is imperceptible.
For a 24-bit color image, 3 bits can be hidden per pixel (one in R, G, and B channels).
Limitation: Low capacity relative to image size (1 bit per channel per pixel).
Limitation: Vulnerable to statistical steganalysis that detects anomalies in bit-plane distributions.
Limitation: Not robust against image compression (JPEG) or transformations that alter pixel values.

questions.question: Discuss the advantages of combining cryptography and steganography for secure communication.
keyPoints: Steganography hides the existence of the message, preventing adversaries from knowing that secret communication is occurring.
Cryptography protects the content, so even if the hidden message is discovered, it remains unreadable without the key.
Combined approach provides defense in depth: the attacker must first detect the hidden message (beat steganalysis), then break the encryption.
This is more secure than either technique alone.

questions.question: Describe the three requirements of a good steganographic system and explain the fundamental trade-off involved.
keyPoints: Imperceptibility: The stego object must be perceptually indistinguishable from the original cover medium.
Capacity: The system must be able to embed enough data to be practically useful.
Robustness: The hidden message should survive common media operations like compression, resizing, and format conversion.
Trade-off: Increasing capacity requires more modifications to the carrier, which reduces imperceptibility and makes detection more likely.
A good system must balance these competing requirements based on the specific application needs.
